While reviewing Alameda County records involving
Supervisor Mary King, I found that I may have ensnared
nyself by failing to report a gift. 
The issue has to do
with a bond rating trip that I took, along with several
other officials, to New York City in November 1988. While
recently revierring records of this trip that were not
available at the tine I retired fron the Authority, I
discovered that I nay have received benefits in the form of
entertainrnent that exceeded the reporting threshold of 950.
Section A72OO-A72LO of the political Reforn Act
requires that etected officials file an annual report that
states his or her interest in real property and his or her
income. Section 87207 defines the nature of reportable
gifts and income. A gift nust be reported if iL amounts to
$50 or more in value.

sections.question: 1. Since I believed that I received less than $50 from Grigsby, Brandford, powell during this trip, have I violated the ASLII? 2. Since the Authority has not made a public determination of what portion of the expenses they will require Mr. Grigsby to pay, how can I be compelled to amend my Form 711 for 1988? 3. Since, in my opinion, I did not received anything of value from Mr. Grigsby beyond a free theater ticket, am I required to report any of the nightclub expenses, including the cost of getting to and from these locations? 4. What happens if the Authority decides that the expenses in question were properly charged to it? Would I still be required to report a gift, or would it be considered reimbursement for normal travel expenses under Section 82030(b)(2)?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks guidance on whether entertainment expenses from a 1988 bond rating trip to New York City constitute reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act, and how to properly amend disclosure filings given uncertainty about the actual costs and the Authority's determination of improper billing.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document is poorly scanned with OCR errors throughout. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections exist; the questions are embedded in numbered list format near end of document. The requester provides background facts and specific statutory references (Sections 87200-87210, 87207, 82030(b)(2), 82028(b)(2)) to support inquiry.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1. Since I believed that I received less than $50 from Grigsby, Brandford, powell during this trip, have I violated the ASLII? 2. Since the Authority has not made a public determination of what portion of the expenses they will require Mr. Grigsby to pay, how can I be compelled to amend my Form 711 for 1988? 3. Since, in my opinion, I did not received anything of value from Mr. Grigsby beyond a free theater ticket, am I required to report any of the nightclub expenses, including the cost of getting to and from these locations? 4. What happens if the Authority decides that the expenses in question were properly charged to it? Would I still be required to report a gift, or would it be considered reimbursement for normal travel expenses under Section 82030(b)(2)?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from R.O. Knox, former Chairperson of the Alameda County Transportation Authority, seeking FPPC ruling on whether entertainment expenses (theater, nightclub, dinner) from a November 1988 trip to New York City must be reported as gifts under the Political Reform Act's $50 reporting threshold, given discrepancies between his initial understanding and later-discovered Authority records.
